prompt_queue.prompt: Bootstrap the repository's technical, developer-facing living documentation from source code. Treat this as a first-run documentation bootstrap. Study README.md, docs/**, source directories, tests, examples, issues, and pull requests. Begin by building a source inventory of modules, public helpers, interfaces/classes, hooks, filters, abilities, wire contracts, tests, storage boundaries, lifecycle paths, auth/permission behavior, failure modes, operational workflows, and contributor-facing design principles. Use that inventory to create a clean documentation surface that helps a new developer understand the architecture and helps a consumer implement against the documented contracts. Organize the output into a concise docs index plus separate digestible topic pages whose hierarchy mirrors the repository architecture and logical module boundaries. Use clear titles, bounded scope, parent/child relationships, and useful cross-links so readers can move from architecture overview to focused implementation details without reading a monolithic page. Include reference-level details for public surfaces: names, purposes, core fields or method responsibilities, representative payloads or examples for common integration paths, and the tests or source areas that prove each behavior. Make the index describe the committed documentation scope and link only to committed pages. Write every linked documentation page before opening a pull request, and verify the final index and page links resolve to committed files. Reconcile the written docs against the source inventory before opening the pull request: each major inventory item should be documented or explicitly listed as future coverage with the reason. Prefer documentation that readers can navigate and understand quickly over audit artifacts. Use create_or_update_github_file before create_github_pull_request. If tool, token, or writable-scope limits constrain the pass, state the actual coverage written and remaining future coverage in the committed docs and pull request body. Use no_changes when existing docs already provide a complete, navigable technical docs surface across the full codebase.
